IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the important practical problem of producing substitutability-oriented sense inventories and shows tangible gains (greater coverage than WordNet and improved substitutability vs prior PPDB clustering and WordNet), which is likely to influence work on lexical substitution and sense-aware NLP components. However, limitations—weak noun performance, reliance on an imperfect best-fit WSD, and no mention of released code—reduce immediate uptake and reproducibility. The method (multi-view NMF with silhouette-driven k selection) is a useful, reasonably general technique that can open follow-up work (better WSD, scalability, and ablations), but as presented it is more of a solid, usable contribution than a field-changing one. | B(3): The paper tackles an important and practical problem—producing sense inventories optimized for substitutability—and demonstrates clear gains over WordNet and prior automatic clusters (PPDBClus) with higher coverage and improved lexsub rankings using oracle and best-fit filtering. These contributions make it a useful resource for lexical substitution and sense-aware applications and open concrete follow-up work on better WSD and noun handling. However, limitations (weaker performance on nouns, reliance on a simple best-fit WSD, and heuristic PPDB cutoffs) mean the work is more likely to be a valuable incremental advance and resource than a field-transforming shift.

However, limitations—weak noun performance, reliance on an imperfect best-fit WSD, and no mention of released code—reduce immediate uptake and reproducibility. The method (multi-view NMF with silhouette-driven k selection) is a useful, reasonably general technique that can open follow-up work (better WSD, scalability, and ablations), but as presented it is more of a solid, usable contribution than a field-changing one.

However, limitations (weaker performance on nouns, reliance on a simple best-fit WSD, and heuristic PPDB cutoffs) mean the work is more likely to be a valuable incremental advance and resource than a field-transforming shift.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from motivation through multi-view NMF methodology to experiments and conclusions; the main contributions (SubstClus inventories and their benefit for lexical substitution) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (e.g., coverage and GAP comparisons, Silhouette-based k selection) are informative. A few presentation gaps remain: the paper does not state code availability, and the description of the best-fit WSD failure modes and oracle vs best-fit experimental setup could be slightly clearer for full reproducibility. | B(4): Overall the paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to multi-view NMF method, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (SubstClus inventories and gains for lexsub ranking) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described as consistent and thorough, and figures/tables/examples seem effective in conveying results (coverage and GAP comparisons). The writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage, though a few method details (e.g., limitations of the simple best-fit WSD and noun-specific weaknesses) could be explained more clearly for readers focused on linguistic interpretability.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from motivation through multi-view NMF methodology to experiments and conclusions; the main contributions (SubstClus inventories and their benefit for lexical substitution) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables (e.g., coverage and GAP comparisons, Silhouette-based k selection) are informative. A few presentation gaps remain: the paper does not state code availability, and the description of the best-fit WSD failure modes and oracle vs best-fit experimental setup could be slightly clearer for full reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper appears well-organized with a clear flow from problem motivation to multi-view NMF method, experiments, and conclusion, and its main contributions (SubstClus inventories and gains for lexsub ranking) are stated early. Notation and hyperparameter reporting are described as consistent and thorough, and figures/tables/examples seem effective in conveying results (coverage and GAP comparisons). The writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage, though a few method details (e.g., limitations of the simple best-fit WSD and noun-specific weaknesses) could be explained more clearly for readers focused on linguistic interpretability.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (sense induction for lexical substitution) using a methodologically appropriate multi-view NMF approach and standard evaluation metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, Silhouette) on established datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword), which aligns well with top-tier NLP/ML venues. The comparison to relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) and reported empirical gains—along with discussion of limitations (noun performance, WSD best-fit failures)—target the right community and audience. From an experimental-method perspective the work fits the venue’s expectations for empirical rigor and relevance to core lexical semantics and downstream lexsub tasks. | B(5): This work sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it targets lexical substitution and sense induction, uses standard datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword) and established metrics (GAP, B-Cubed, Silhouette), and compares against appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, AddCos). The problem formulation (building substitutability-tailored sense inventories and evaluating their utility for lexsub) and multi-view NMF methodology meet community expectations for methodological rigor and evaluation. The paper is clearly aimed at the NLP research audience interested in sense representation and practical lexical resources, and is positioned with thorough related work coverage relevant to that community.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (sense induction for lexical substitution) using a methodologically appropriate multi-view NMF approach and standard evaluation metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, Silhouette) on established datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword), which aligns well with top-tier NLP/ML venues. The comparison to relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) and reported empirical gains—along with discussion of limitations (noun performance, WSD best-fit failures)—target the right community and audience. From an experimental-method perspective the work fits the venue’s expectations for empirical rigor and relevance to core lexical semantics and downstream lexsub tasks.

reviewer_b.justification: This work sits squarely within the scope of a top-tier NLP venue: it targets lexical substitution and sense induction, uses standard datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword) and established metrics (GAP, B-Cubed, Silhouette), and compares against appropriate baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, AddCos). The problem formulation (building substitutability-tailored sense inventories and evaluating their utility for lexsub) and multi-view NMF methodology meet community expectations for methodological rigor and evaluation. The paper is clearly aimed at the NLP research audience interested in sense representation and practical lexical resources, and is positioned with thorough related work coverage relevant to that community.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) using the same datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword) and appropriate metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, Silhouette). It transparently acknowledges when prior methods outperform it (TWSI on nouns) and analyzes oracle vs. best-fit filtering effects. Minor gaps remain around statistical significance reporting and potential new neural contextual baselines, but overall the comparisons are fair and well-motivated. | B(5): The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus from Cocos & Callison‑Burch 2016, Syn.VSM, AddCos) and uses the same datasets and evaluation metrics (CoInCo, GAP, B‑Cubed) for fair comparison. It explicitly analyzes when prior methods outperform the proposal (notably TWSI on nouns), reports oracle vs. best‑fit filtering to unpack differences, and discusses choices like PPDB score cutoffs; related work coverage is described as thorough. From a lexical‑semantics perspective this constitutes a comprehensive and fair experimental comparison with the most pertinent competing approaches.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) using the same datasets (CoInCo, PPDB, Gigaword) and appropriate metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, Silhouette). It transparently acknowledges when prior methods outperform it (TWSI on nouns) and analyzes oracle vs. best-fit filtering effects. Minor gaps remain around statistical significance reporting and potential new neural contextual baselines, but overall the comparisons are fair and well-motivated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong set of relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus from Cocos & Callison‑Burch 2016, Syn.VSM, AddCos) and uses the same datasets and evaluation metrics (CoInCo, GAP, B‑Cubed) for fair comparison. It explicitly analyzes when prior methods outperform the proposal (notably TWSI on nouns), reports oracle vs. best‑fit filtering to unpack differences, and discusses choices like PPDB score cutoffs; related work coverage is described as thorough. From a lexical‑semantics perspective this constitutes a comprehensive and fair experimental comparison with the most pertinent competing approaches.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper's main claims are reasonably supported by experiments showing higher coverage and substitutability of SubstClus vs. WordNet and PPDBClus and by gains in GAP under oracle and best-fit filtering; the methodology (multi-view NMF, silhouette-based k selection, and lexsub evaluations) is appropriate. However, I see missing evidence on statistical significance and runtime/scalability (per-target NMF and cost of multiple views) and no mention of released code for reproducibility, which weakens trust in the empirical claims. The authors themselves note failure modes (best-fit WSD errors, weaker noun performance), indicating some unsupported generality of improvements. Overall the work is promising but has gaps in experimental rigor and reproducibility that preclude a higher technical score. | B(4): The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence across relevant datasets (CoInCo) and baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), showing improved coverage and substitutability and gains in lexsub ranking using oracle and best-fit filtering. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify beyond standard multi-view NMF and Silhouette-based model selection, which are appropriate and appear correctly applied. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with proper baselines and ablations, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the simple best-fit WSD is admitted to be a weak component—small methodological concerns that do not overturn the results. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented analyses and acknowledged limitations (e.g., weaker noun performance and WSD failures).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are reasonably supported by experiments showing higher coverage and substitutability of SubstClus vs. WordNet and PPDBClus and by gains in GAP under oracle and best-fit filtering; the methodology (multi-view NMF, silhouette-based k selection, and lexsub evaluations) is appropriate. However, I see missing evidence on statistical significance and runtime/scalability (per-target NMF and cost of multiple views) and no mention of released code for reproducibility, which weakens trust in the empirical claims. The authors themselves note failure modes (best-fit WSD errors, weaker noun performance), indicating some unsupported generality of improvements. Overall the work is promising but has gaps in experimental rigor and reproducibility that preclude a higher technical score.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported by experimental evidence across relevant datasets (CoInCo) and baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), showing improved coverage and substitutability and gains in lexsub ranking using oracle and best-fit filtering. There are no complex mathematical derivations to verify beyond standard multi-view NMF and Silhouette-based model selection, which are appropriate and appear correctly applied. The experimental methodology is broadly sound with proper baselines and ablations, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and the simple best-fit WSD is admitted to be a weak component—small methodological concerns that do not overturn the results. Overall the conclusions are supported by the presented analyses and acknowledged limitations (e.g., weaker noun performance and WSD failures).

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable, if not radical, novelty: framing sense induction for substitutability as a multi-view NMF clustering problem that integrates context-based AddCos, paraphrase scores, translations, and WordNet affinities, and uses Silhouette-based k selection. These elements clearly differentiate it from single-view or paraphrase-only prior work (e.g., PPDBClus, TWSI) and constitute a non-trivial methodological extension rather than a mere reapplication. From an expert perspective the multi-view formulation and practical clustering choices (per-target NMF, k selection) would be informative and surprising relative to prior art, though they build on existing components rather than introducing an entirely new theoretical paradigm. | B(4): The paper offers a notable and well-differentiated contribution: constructing substitutability-oriented sense inventories via multi-view NMF over context-based AddCos, PPDB paraphrases, translations, and WordNet affinity, which goes beyond single-source clustering like PPDBClus (Cocos & Callison-Burch) and prior VSM approaches. Selecting k by Silhouette and explicitly optimizing for substitutability (and showing gains in lexsub when used as filters) frames a clear new perspective compared to standard WSI and paraphrase clustering work. While components (NMF, PPDB, cross-lingual signals) are known, their integrated multi-view use specifically targeted at substitutability and inventory construction is a non-trivial and informative extension that would interest experts in lexical semantics.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable, if not radical, novelty: framing sense induction for substitutability as a multi-view NMF clustering problem that integrates context-based AddCos, paraphrase scores, translations, and WordNet affinities, and uses Silhouette-based k selection. These elements clearly differentiate it from single-view or paraphrase-only prior work (e.g., PPDBClus, TWSI) and constitute a non-trivial methodological extension rather than a mere reapplication. From an expert perspective the multi-view formulation and practical clustering choices (per-target NMF, k selection) would be informative and surprising relative to prior art, though they build on existing components rather than introducing an entirely new theoretical paradigm.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper offers a notable and well-differentiated contribution: constructing substitutability-oriented sense inventories via multi-view NMF over context-based AddCos, PPDB paraphrases, translations, and WordNet affinity, which goes beyond single-source clustering like PPDBClus (Cocos & Callison-Burch) and prior VSM approaches. Selecting k by Silhouette and explicitly optimizing for substitutability (and showing gains in lexsub when used as filters) frames a clear new perspective compared to standard WSI and paraphrase clustering work. While components (NMF, PPDB, cross-lingual signals) are known, their integrated multi-view use specifically targeted at substitutability and inventory construction is a non-trivial and informative extension that would interest experts in lexical semantics.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The experimental setup is reasonably thorough: multiple realistic baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), relevant datasets (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, Gigaword), and evaluation metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, silhouette) supporting the main claims. However, important analyses appear missing or underreported in the summary: I see no explicit ablation isolating the contribution of each view in the multi‑view NMF or sensitivity analyses for key hyperparameters (beyond k selection by silhouette), and the simple best‑fit WSD’s failures suggest a need for deeper diagnostics. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (weaker noun performance, best‑fit vs oracle gap, PPDB cutoff effects), but reproducibility details are incomplete in the summary (code availability, random seeds, dataset splits, runtimes, and statistical significance testing not mentioned). Given solid comparative experiments but these notable gaps, I rate it as adequate (3) pending those additional analyses and replication materials. | B(4): The experiments are strong: multiple relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) are compared on appropriate metrics (B-Cubed, GAP) and both oracle and best-fit filtering are evaluated, supporting the claim that SubstClus improves substitutability and lexsub filtering. Sensitivity analyses are present in part (Silhouette-based k selection, PPDB score cutoff) but there is no explicit multi-view or component ablation reported in the summary to show which views drive gains. Related work coverage appears thorough, and important limitations are discussed (weaker performance on nouns, best-fit WSD failures). Reproducibility is partially addressed (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, leaving some practical gaps for full replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental setup is reasonably thorough: multiple realistic baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos), relevant datasets (CoInCo, PPDB2.0, Gigaword), and evaluation metrics (B-Cubed F, GAP, silhouette) supporting the main claims. However, important analyses appear missing or underreported in the summary: I see no explicit ablation isolating the contribution of each view in the multi‑view NMF or sensitivity analyses for key hyperparameters (beyond k selection by silhouette), and the simple best‑fit WSD’s failures suggest a need for deeper diagnostics. The paper does acknowledge some limitations (weaker noun performance, best‑fit vs oracle gap, PPDB cutoff effects), but reproducibility details are incomplete in the summary (code availability, random seeds, dataset splits, runtimes, and statistical significance testing not mentioned). Given solid comparative experiments but these notable gaps, I rate it as adequate (3) pending those additional analyses and replication materials.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong: multiple relevant baselines (WordNet, TWSI, PPDBClus, Syn.VSM, AddCos) are compared on appropriate metrics (B-Cubed, GAP) and both oracle and best-fit filtering are evaluated, supporting the claim that SubstClus improves substitutability and lexsub filtering. Sensitivity analyses are present in part (Silhouette-based k selection, PPDB score cutoff) but there is no explicit multi-view or component ablation reported in the summary to show which views drive gains. Related work coverage appears thorough, and important limitations are discussed (weaker performance on nouns, best-fit WSD failures). Reproducibility is partially addressed (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, leaving some practical gaps for full replication.

main_claims: Introduces a multi-view clustering method to produce sense inventories tailored for substitutability.
The resulting SubstClus sense inventories have greater coverage than WordNet and are more substitutable than WordNet and prior automatically clustered paraphrases (Cocos & Callison-Burch, 2016).
Using these sense inventories as filters improves lexical substitution rankings (GAP) of existing lexsub ranking models; oracle and best-fit filtering both show gains.
_summary.methodology: Builds sense clusters for each target by multi-view nonnegative matrix factorization over multiple views (Context Substitutability using AddCos scores from sentence contexts, Paraphrase Similarity using PPDB2.0 scores, Shared Translations, and WordNet synset affinity), selects number of clusters k by maximizing Silhouette Coefficient, and uses the clusters to filter lexsub rankings via an oracle and a simple best-fit WSD.
limitations: SubstClus inventories are weakest for improving lexsub rankings for nouns; TWSI outperforms them on nouns.
The simple best-fit WSD method frequently fails to find the best sense cluster, so Best-Fit GAP is not perfectly correlated with Oracle GAP.
Applying a PPDB score cutoff (2.3) reduces coverage to decrease noise.

reviewer_a.name: Experimental Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics; 8+ years building and evaluating NLP systems in both academia and industry with emphasis on reproducibility and empirical rigor.
traits: notices whether baselines are implemented and compared fairly (same data, preprocessing, and hyperparameter search)
requires clear ablation studies and sensitivity analyses for algorithmic components and hyperparameters (e.g., impact of each view, choice of k, silhouette selection)
values reproducible experimental protocol: seeds, dataset splits, runtime, implementation details, and availability of code and data
looks for proper statistical treatment of results (confidence intervals, significance tests, evaluation metrics aligned with claims)
checks computational cost and scalability (time, memory) relative to baselines and practicality of methods like NMF per-target
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I focus on the experimental setup, ablations, and reproducibility: I will ask for concrete additional experiments, diagnostics, and clarifications needed to trust the empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Lexical Semantics & Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor researching lexical semantics and sense representation; extensive publication record on word sense induction, substitution, and lexical resources integration in downstream NLP applications.
traits: evaluates novelty and positioning relative to prior sense induction and lexical substitution literature (e.g., WSI, PPDB-based methods, sense inventories)
assesses whether the proposed sense inventories meet practical needs for downstream tasks and resource-building (coverage, interpretability, human plausibility)
notices connections to linguistic notions (substitutability vs. synonymy) and whether evaluation metrics reflect those notions
values broader impact: whether results shift the field (e.g., providing better inventories than WordNet) and clarity about intended use-cases and limitations
looks for qualitative analysis: examples, failure modes, human evaluation or annotation to support claims about substitutability and interpretability
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge the paper on novelty, conceptual framing, and real-world significance: I will ask for stronger narrative and qualitative evidence connecting the method to linguistic goals and downstream benefits.
